featuredSetups.label: Room to dissolve
featuredSetups.description: Open a beaker that still has room for more solute under the current conditions.

featuredSetups.label: At the limit
featuredSetups.description: Open a saturated beaker with visible excess solid.

pageIntro.definition: Use one beaker to see solubility as a capacity limit: what dissolves, what remains as excess solid, and what changes when capacity grows.
pageIntro.whyItMatters: It keeps solubility separate from concentration, so saturation becomes a visible limit you can reason with instead of a rule to memorize.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: A saturated solution has reached its current dissolving capacity; extra solute stays undissolved until solvent volume or the solubility limit increases.

paragraphs: Solubility is the current cap on how much solute can stay dissolved. Saturation is what you see when that cap is reached and the extra material has to remain visible instead of quietly dissolving.
This bench keeps dissolved amount, excess solid, and capacity in the same beaker so concentration and solubility stay connected without collapsing into the same idea.

keyIdeas: Concentration describes how much is already dissolved per amount of solvent.
Solubility describes the current maximum that can stay dissolved under the current conditions.
Once the beaker saturates, adding more solute changes the excess pile more than the dissolved amount.

commonMisconception.myth: If more solid is visible, the solution must always be more concentrated.
correction: Visible solid can simply mean the system is saturated and has run out of dissolving capacity.
A saturated beaker can hold the same dissolved amount even while the total solute keeps increasing.

workedExamples.title: Live worked example
workedExamples.intro: Read the live beaker state rather than a detached table.

items.title: Read the current capacity
items.prompt: For the current mixture, how much solute can stay dissolved before the beaker saturates?

steps.label: 1. Read the current limit
steps.template: The current solubility limit is {{solubilityLimitValue}} units per volume.

steps.label: 2. Use the solvent volume to find the current capacity
steps.template: With {{solventVolumeValue}} units of solvent, the beaker can currently hold about {{capacityValue}} units of dissolved solute.

steps.label: 3. Compare the total solute with the capacity
steps.template: The beaker contains {{soluteAmountValue}} units of solute, so the undissolved excess is {{excessAmountValue}} units whenever the total rises above capacity.

applyAction.label: Apply the near-saturated case
applyAction.observationHint: The dissolved amount should sit close to the current capacity while the excess pile starts to become visible.

miniChallenge.prompt: Make the excess solid disappear without removing any solute from the beaker.
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether the cleaner move is to add solvent or to raise the solubility limit before you try it.
miniChallenge.answer: You can add solvent or raise the solubility limit so more of the same solute can stay dissolved.
miniChallenge.explanation: Both moves increase capacity. Removing solute would be a different chemistry story.

equations.label: Capacity rule
equations.meaning: Capacity depends on the current solubility limit and the amount of solvent.

equations.label: Dissolved amount
equations.meaning: The dissolved amount cannot rise above the current capacity.

equations.label: Excess solid
equations.meaning: Any solute above the current capacity stays visible as undissolved material.

equations.label: Concentration
equations.meaning: Concentration only counts what is actually dissolved in the solvent.

variableLinks.label: Solute amount
variableLinks.description: Changes how much material the beaker is trying to dissolve.

variableLinks.label: Solvent volume
variableLinks.description: Changes how much liquid is available to hold dissolved solute.

variableLinks.label: Solubility limit
variableLinks.description: Changes how much can stay dissolved under the current conditions.

presets.label: Room to dissolve
presets.description: A case where the dissolved amount is still below the current capacity.

presets.label: Near saturation
presets.description: A case where the dissolved amount is almost at the current limit.

presets.label: At the limit
presets.description: A saturated case with visible excess solid.

overlays.label: Saturation gauge
overlays.shortDescription: Show the current capacity and how close the beaker is to the limit.
whatToNotice: The dissolved amount can stop increasing even when the total solute keeps rising.
overlays.whyItMatters: It makes the solubility limit visible as a real capacity instead of a slogan.

overlays.label: Dissolved cue
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the dissolved part of the mixture easy to spot.
whatToNotice: The dissolved amount and the excess solid are not the same thing.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps concentration tied to the dissolved portion instead of the total material on the bench.

overlays.label: Excess pile
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the undissolved extra material visible when the beaker saturates.
whatToNotice: Extra solute does not disappear once the current capacity is full.
overlays.whyItMatters: It shows why saturation is different from a simple concentration increase.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the beaker and the saturation gauge in view together.

items.text: Watch for the point where the dissolved amount stops climbing and the excess pile starts to appear.
items.tryThis: Raise the solute amount a little from the room-to-dissolve setup until the first extra solid becomes visible.
items.whyItMatters: That is the moment the solution saturates under the current conditions.

items.text: Changing the solubility limit changes the capacity itself, not just the concentration readout.
items.tryThis: Increase the solubility limit and watch how the same beaker moves farther below the saturation threshold.
items.whyItMatters: It keeps solubility distinct from concentration.

predictionMode.title: Prediction mode
predictionMode.intro: Predict how the capacity changes before you apply it.

items.prompt: What should happen if you raise the solubility limit while keeping the same solute amount and solvent volume?

choices.label: More of the same solute can stay dissolved, so the excess pile shrinks or disappears.
The dissolved amount must stay the same because the total solute has not changed.
The beaker becomes more saturated because the limit is higher.

items.explanation: A higher solubility limit increases capacity, so more of the same total solute can stay dissolved.
items.observationHint: Watch the saturation gauge and the excess pile together after the limit increase.

quickTest.title: Quick test
quickTest.intro: Answer from the live beaker story instead of a memorized slogan.

questions.prompt: What does saturation most directly mean on this bench?

choices.label: The dissolved amount has reached the current capacity, so extra solute stays undissolved.
Any beaker with visible particles is automatically saturated.
Saturation only describes how concentrated the dissolved part is.

questions.explanation: Saturation is about the current limit on what can stay dissolved.

questions.prompt: Which idea tells you the maximum that can stay dissolved under the current conditions?
questions.explanation: Concentration describes what is already dissolved. Solubility tells you the capacity.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use capacity honestly: do not hide the excess by removing the solute that caused it.

items.title: Dissolve the excess without removing solute
items.prompt: Starting from a saturated beaker, make the excess pile disappear while keeping the same total solute amount.
items.successMessage: You increased the capacity enough for the same total solute to stay dissolved instead of silently removing material from the problem.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a solution beaker, a saturation gauge, and a visible excess-solid cue alongside controls for solute amount, solvent volume, and the current solubility limit.
A readout card reports the total solute, current capacity, dissolved amount, excess solid, and concentration so the learner can connect the visual bench to the numeric summary.

One graph shows how the dissolved amount changes as total solute rises, another shows how the excess solid grows after saturation, and two more show how capacity changes with solvent volume and solubility limit.
Graph hover and compare mode stay attached to the same chemistry bench instead of opening a separate chemistry-only view.

seo.title: Solubility and Saturation
seo.description: Explore solubility, saturation, dissolved amount, and excess solid in one bounded chemistry beaker.

v2.intuition: Use one beaker to see solubility as a capacity limit: what dissolves, what remains as excess solid, and what changes when capacity grows.
v2.whyItMatters: It keeps solubility separate from concentration, so saturation becomes a visible limit you can reason with instead of a rule to memorize.

guidedSteps.title: Read available capacity
guidedSteps.goal: Use one beaker to separate what is dissolved already from how much more can still fit.
guidedSteps.doThis: Start with the room-to-dissolve preset and raise Solute amount while the dissolved cue stays visible.
guidedSteps.notice: All of the added solute still dissolves because the beaker has not reached its current capacity yet.
guidedSteps.explain: Before saturation, the dissolved amount can keep rising because the amount added is still below the capacity set by the solvent volume and solubility limit.

guidedSteps.title: Hit the saturation limit
guidedSteps.goal: Watch extra solute stop dissolving once the beaker reaches capacity.
guidedSteps.doThis: Apply the at-the-limit preset, then push Solute amount beyond the current limit.
guidedSteps.notice: The dissolved amount stops climbing while an excess pile starts to grow.
guidedSteps.explain: Saturation is a capacity limit. Once the dissolved amount has reached that limit, extra solute remains undissolved instead of raising the dissolved concentration further.

guidedSteps.title: Remove excess by changing capacity
guidedSteps.goal: See how extra solid can disappear without removing any solute from the system.
guidedSteps.doThis: Keep the same loaded beaker, then increase Solvent volume or Solubility limit until the excess pile disappears.
guidedSteps.notice: The excess solid can shrink even when no solute is removed because the beaker can now dissolve more.
guidedSteps.explain: Changing capacity is different from changing concentration directly. More solvent or a higher solubility limit increases how much solute the liquid can hold.

learned: Solubility sets a capacity limit for how much solute can dissolve under the current conditions.
At saturation, extra solute stops entering solution and stays as excess solid.
Changing solvent volume or the solubility limit can remove excess without taking solute away.
wrapUp.misconception: Once a beaker is saturated, the dissolved concentration must keep rising if you keep adding solute.

nextConcepts.title: Acid-Base / pH Intuition
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Carry solution concentration into pH and acid-base reasoning

nextConcepts.title: Buffers and Neutralization
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Use concentration and capacity ideas in reaction contexts

wrapUp.freePlayPrompt: Build one beaker with visible excess and another that dissolves the same total solute by changing capacity.
